longSentences.text: but it's now 2026, which means we're already halfway towards the next games, and this one has set some pretty ambitious targets. There will be zero new venues, and it's being positioned as a nocar event. So, remind me where's this being held again?

>> Moving the hashtag from 2024 to 2028, Los Angeles has decided it can wait it out an extra four years. When LA was awarded the 2028 Olympics back in 2017, the situation was far from normal. Paris was given the 2024 games at the very same event. Two host cities had never before been announced together. But there were good reasons for this.

>> It became very clear that the IOC was having enormous difficulties finding cities that wanted to host the games. And indeed, the potential hosting for the 2024 games had four cities, four cities that had been bidding to host the games

drop out. When they made their decision in 2017, there were only two biders left and one was Paris and one was Los Angeles. They were both good bets. Although Paris got 2024, LA was still a strong contender and agreed to wait another four years in return for extra funding, which was helpful because the next tournament is expected to cost around $7 billion.

What made America's Second City a good bet for 2028 wasn't just that it would avoid another awkward round of bids from a dwindling number of candidates later on. No, it's the fact that it's already been there and done it twice.

The first occasion was way back in 1932, taking place during the Great Depression. There were half as many athletes competing compared to Amsterdam 4 years earlier. But that didn't stop a then record-breaking 100,000 people turning up for the opening ceremony.

Fast forward half a century, and LA 1984 once again saw fewer countries competing, this time due to a boycott from the Soviet block. And yet you had Carl Lewis winning four gold medals, Michael Jordan top scoring in the basketball, and incredibly, the games finished with an actual profit of over $250 million.

2028 will see Los Angeles become the first city to hold the Olympics three times, and it'll continue a trend set by the two previous hosts. Both Paris and Tokyo focused on reusing venues rather than building lots of new ones. Not only did that reduce cost, it also avoided them spending huge amounts of money on giant arenas that could go on to become white elephants like we've seen in the past. Paris set the bar with 95% of its stadiums already existing. But LA plans to raise it even further, going ahead without any new construction whatsoever.

Take the brand new into it dome now home to the Clippers of the NBA. No prizes for guessing. That's where you're going to find the basketball. Dodger Stadium is going to be for yeah baseball. And the wittier narrows Clay Shooting Center isn't going to be hosting the dress arch.

While some of the venue choices are obvious, not all of them are being used in the way you might think, and others need a fair bit of upgrading before they're ready for the 2028 games. Take the new SoFi Stadium, primarily built for NFL and now set to host FIFA World Cup matches in 2026 along with the Super Bowl in 2027.

But in 2028, SoFi won't be hosting the footy or American football, which isn't even in the Olympics. Instead, fans are going to come here to watch, wait for it, swimming. The most expensive stadium ever built is going to be converted into the largest dedicated aquatics venue in Olympics history with 40,000 seats.

Now, the exact details of how they plan to do that aren't known yet, but obviously it's going to involve constructing a large pool in the middle of the stadium, one that will have to be removed again before the NFL season starts later that year.

It'll be a massive undertaking, but Paris proved this sort of thing can be done. This temporary pool built for the 2024 games was constructed in just over a month. Doing something similar at Sofi is going to put swimming on a much bigger platform than when LA last hosted the Olympics. I like the the venue that they had at USC at University of Southern California, uh, which they used in 1984, but they felt like it it didn't provide the environment that was ideal.

The original idea was for SoFi to hold the closing ceremony as well with the LA Memorial Coliseum handling the opener, but plans have changed slightly. The opening ceremony is now going to be co-hosted by both stadiums. In fact, the Colosseum is another iconic venue that's set to undergo a bit of a transformation because like most Olympic stadiums, it's also going to be used for athletics.

Overall, there'll be more than 40 venues spread across the city from Santa Monica to Anaheim and from Pasadena to Long Beach. As for getting to these sites, you might think that with this being LA, people are just going to pull up in their cars. But according to Mayor Karen Bass, the plan is actually for people to do the exact opposite.

>> The main push will be no cars to the venues. Bass says the Olympics are a chance to make transformative change for the future. Okay, but because many people will still consider LA to be a very car- centric city, that idea sounds kind of crazy. Or maybe not, because since the last time LA hosted the Olympics, the cities introduced a completely new way of getting around the metro system. In 1990, just 6 years after the event, the city unveiled its first line, finally giving Angelinos the option to travel by rail instead of road.

Was that promise kept? Well, fast forward a quarter of a century and the network looks like this, with well over a 100 miles of routes now stretching across the city. While much of that work took place years ago, quite a lot's been done to this piece of infrastructure since the LA 2028 announcement. As of June 2025, the rail system has finally been linked up to the airport on the K and C lines. An automated people mover, also long awaited, is set to follow on in 2026. That'll provide a new direct rail link between the new Metro Transit Center and the LAX terminals.

Another example of recent progress is the southern section of the Blue Line, now called the A- Line, heading straight out of Compton down to Long Beach, the route was given upgraded tracks, power lines, switching, and control systems back in 2019.

Then there's the East Side Transit Corridor, which is going to link downtown to four of the so-called gateway cities in Southeast LA County. The first phase connecting Little Tokyo to the Atlantic Station in East Los Angeles as part of the Eline completed back in 2023.

>> He's referring here to the Dline extension. It's going to run from Union Station through one of the city's biggest retail and commercial districts all the way out to Westside. >> That's a highcapacity system in a very dense corridor and it will move a ton of people much faster than buses can um and much obviously much more effectively than than cars.

Two tunnel boring machines spent 5 years digging the 14 km new line, which is set to carry around 30,000 people a day. One LA Metro called it one of the most complex engineering feats the city's transit authority has ever undertaken.

As workers excavated around 50 ft of tunnel each day, they discovered obstacles like gassy ground, tar sands, and abandoned oil wells. Thankfully, passengers are going to move through it a lot faster when it's in operation as they travel from downtown to West LA in less than 30 minutes.

These works are all part of what's called the 28x 28 initiative announced by the city's former mayor in 2018. It's a list of, yep, 28 transport projects that the city of Los Angeles planned to complete in time for the games. All due to cost more than $26 billion in total. So far, around a third of them are in operation.

Those who still insist on driving are going to have to park away from the venues or travel by Uber, bike, or scooter via one of the planned mobility hubs.
